steps.title: Find the two independent clauses
steps.content: Clause 1: "Before the park's construction, the surface temperature 30 meters outside of the park boundary was 0.1°C cooler than that of a control area." Clause 2: "After construction, the temperature 30 meters outside of the boundary was 1.7°C cooler than that of the control area." Both can stand alone.

steps.title: Pick the right punctuation
steps.content: Two independent clauses must be joined by a period, a semicolon, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction. A period before "After" works perfectly.

steps.title: Eliminate the wrong choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘area after’: runs the two independent clauses together with no punctuation — run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘area after,’: a comma after "after" awkwardly separates the introductory word from its clause and still creates a run-on between the two main clauses. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘area. After’: period correctly ends clause 1; "After construction" begins clause 2 with a proper intro phrase. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘area, after’: comma splice — a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
